The Artemis 2 mission is generating exactly the response you'd expect from the 'truth-seeking community'. It's all fake. It's all CGI. Nobody went to the moon in 1969 and nobody's going now. Case closed.

And maybe they're right. I have my best guesses, but I genuinely don't know for sure. But what I do know is that the speed and certainty with which people dismiss it tells me more about the psychology of the person than it does about the event itself. And that is what I am interested in.

When someone watches a rocket launch and their immediate response is "fake" with the same unshakable conviction that the mainstream has when they say "trust the science"... what exactly is the difference? You've replaced one set of unquestionable conclusions with another.

I thought we were here to ask questions. I thought the whole point was to think critically, hold things loosely, and resist the pull of certainty in either direction. When did "I can't know for sure" become taboo when it comes to certain topics?
And what if some of these conspiracy narratives are themselves part of a deeper psychological operation? If you wanted to undermine a civilisation's belief in its own achievements... if you wanted a population to distrust everything it has ever built, created, or accomplished... what better way than to seed the idea that none of it was real?

I'm not telling you what to believe about space or the moon. I'm asking you to notice how you arrived at what you believe... and whether that process looked more like genuine inquiry or like joining another herd that had already decided for you, and who you are not allowed to question.

Even though Artemis 2 will not land on the Moon (it's a ~10-day **free-return flyby** that loops around the Moon and comes straight back to Earth using gravity), the mission delivers substantial technical, scientific, operational, and inspirational benefits. It serves as a critical **crewed test flight** for NASA's deep-space systems before attempting more complex landings.
1. Major Technical and Safety Validation
This is the **first time humans fly aboard the SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft** in deep space—the first crewed mission beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972. Key tests include:
- **Life support systems**: Verifying that Orion can sustain four astronauts with air, water, temperature control, waste management, and other essentials for the full duration in the harsh environment of deep space (far beyond Earth's protective magnetosphere).
- **Radiation protection and monitoring**: Measuring and mitigating cosmic radiation exposure, which is a major challenge for long-duration missions.
- **Navigation, communications, propulsion, and manual control**: Astronauts will test guidance systems, perform maneuvers (including with the European Service Module), and demonstrate they can manually fly Orion if automated systems fail.
- **High-speed re-entry**: Orion will return at nearly 25,000 mph (about 40,000 km/h), testing the heat shield and overall structural integrity under real conditions.
- **Overall spacecraft and ground systems performance**: From launch through recovery, confirming everything works with a crew aboard (building directly on the uncrewed Artemis 1 test).

Crew safety is the top priority—NASA deliberately chose a simpler free-return trajectory so the spacecraft can come home naturally even in case of major issues. Success here greatly reduces risk for future missions.
2. Paving the Way for Lunar Landings and Beyond
Artemis 2 acts as a bridge between the uncrewed Artemis 1 and the crewed landing attempts (Artemis 3 or later, currently targeted for 2028 or so). It proves the core transportation system (SLS + Orion) is ready before integrating more complex elements like SpaceX's Starship lander or the Lunar Gateway station. Data from this flight will directly inform spacecraft design tweaks, procedures, and training for surface missions.

Longer-term, these capabilities support sustained human presence on the Moon (as a testbed for living/working off-Earth) and eventual missions to Mars. Technologies refined here—like life support, power systems, and radiation shielding—apply to deeper space exploration.

3. Scientific and Human Health Research
The crew (Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen) will serve as both pilots and scientists/research subjects. Benefits include:
- **Astronaut health studies**: Experiments on sleep, stress, cognitive performance, immune system changes, teamwork, and radiation/microgravity effects on human tissues (using advanced "organ-on-a-chip" devices).
- **Lunar observations**: From their unique vantage point, astronauts will photograph and document the Moon (including the far side), collecting data to support future landing site selection and lunar science.
- **Broader deep-space research**: Testing tools and workflows that future surface crews will use.

These studies advance our understanding of human physiology in space and help develop countermeasures for longer missions.

4. Operational and Programmatic Benefits
- Validates mission planning, ground operations, and international collaboration (e.g., Canadian astronaut involvement).
- Builds experience and confidence for the entire Artemis team.
- Demonstrates a hybrid government-commercial approach to exploration.

5. Inspirational and Societal Value

Nuclear electric propulsion offers excellent fuel efficiency via continuous low-thrust ion drives powered by a nuclear reactor. But for the short Earth-Moon hop (Artemis 2's ~4-day free-return coast), that low thrust means a slow spiral acceleration from orbit—likely weeks, not days. Chemical rockets are still faster here. NEP shines for deep-space hauls like Mars, slashing transit from 6+ months to as little as 45 days.
